slides.title: Build Your Own Data Types
slides.body: Python gives you strings, lists, and numbers — but what if you need a **Dog**, a **BankAccount**, or a **Player**? A `class` lets you invent any type you need.
slides.tip: Classes bundle data and behavior into one tidy, reusable package.

slides.title: Blueprint vs. The Real Thing
slides.body: A **class** is the cookie cutter — just the shape. An **object** (or *instance*) is an actual cookie stamped from it. Define the class once, create as many objects as you like.
slides.code:
class Dog:
    pass

my_dog = Dog()
print(type(my_dog))

slides.title: `__init__` Sets Up Each Object
slides.body: The special `__init__` method runs automatically the moment you create a new instance. Use it to store each object's data with `self.attribute = value`.
slides.tip: `self` always refers to the specific object being created or used right now.

slides.title: Methods Give Objects Actions
slides.body: A **method** is a function inside a class. It starts with `self` so it can reach the object's own data. Call it with dot notation: `object.method()`.

slides.title: You Get It — Now Go Build!
slides.body: A **class** is a blueprint. `__init__` sets up each object's data. **Methods** define what it can do. Every instance is independent — change one, the others stay untouched.
slides.tip: Classes keep related data and behavior together, making your code easier to read, reuse, and grow.
